gamma_events.title: Highest temperature in Munich on June 23?
gamma_events.description:
This market will resolve to the temperature range that contains the highest temperature recorded at the Munich Airport Station in degrees Celsius on 23 Jun '26.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the Munich Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/de/munich/EDDM.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Current numerical weather prediction models, including recent ECMWF and GFS runs, indicate a warm air mass advecting over southern Germany, supporting a June 23 maximum near 32 °C in Munich with light rain or showers possible midday. This consensus underpins the market’s tight clustering around 31–33 °C, where small differences in cloud timing, boundary-layer mixing, and precipitation onset create the observed spread between leading outcomes. Ensemble variability remains modest two days out, yet traders weigh the risk that earlier or heavier showers could cap the peak at 31 °C versus clearer intervals allowing 33 °C, with official DWD observations on the day serving as the resolution source.

eventMetadata.context_description: Forecast models from agencies like the DWD and ECMWF currently indicate a peak temperature near 32°C in Munich on June 24 under sunny skies and light northeasterly winds, supporting the market's highest implied probability on that outcome. A stable high-pressure system over central Europe has suppressed cloud cover and precipitation in recent days, allowing daytime heating to exceed June climatological averages of 21–24°C. Key variables include potential shifts in steering flow that could introduce more moisture or alter insolation, plus minor model spread on maximum values between 30–34°C. Updated short-range guidance expected in the next 24–48 hours will likely refine these projections ahead of resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: A strengthening high-pressure system and associated heat dome are driving the current market positioning, with official Met Office guidance and ensemble model runs from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts pointing to peak temperatures of 34–38 °C across London on 23 June. Warm advection from the near-continent, combined with clear skies and light winds, favors rapid daytime warming, while recent amber heat-health alerts reflect growing model consensus that 35 °C will be exceeded. Forecaster updates in the past 48 hours have nudged higher-end scenarios toward 37–38 °C in parts of the southeast, yet spread among runs leaves room for a lower outcome if cloud or a slightly stronger sea breeze develops. Final guidance from the Met Office and updated high-resolution runs on 22 June will be the key catalysts likely to shift trader probabilities before resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent Met Office forecasts highlight a strengthening high-pressure system and warm southerly flow driving an early-summer heatwave across southern England, with central London temperatures potentially peaking near 35–37 °C on June 24. Ensemble model runs show modest spread around these values due to uncertain cloud cover and boundary-layer mixing, while climatological baselines place average June maxima near 20–22 °C. The amber extreme-heat warning through mid-week and the proximity to the UK June record of 35.6 °C anchor trader focus on the 36–38 °C band, though any overnight cooling or Atlantic front arrival could cap readings at 35 °C or below. Updated guidance expected within 24 hours will further refine land-breeze and urban-heat-island effects.

eventMetadata.context_description: Forecast models from NOAA and other agencies indicate a high-pressure ridge over the Northeast with light northerly flow and variable cloud cover moderating peak heating in New York City on June 23, keeping the daily maximum in the mid-to-upper 70s°F rather than the seasonal average near 80–83°F. Ensemble spreads arise mainly from differences in predicted boundary-layer mixing, dew points, and the timing of any weak frontal passage, producing the tight clustering of market-implied odds around 76–81°F. Recent short-range guidance updates have shown minimal day-to-day shifts, reflecting stable large-scale steering patterns and limited convective activity that could otherwise boost or suppress temperatures by a few degrees. Traders are weighting these consensus outputs heavily, as resolution will hinge on official Central Park observations.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader sentiment for Tokyo's June 23 maximum temperature centers on the tsuyu rainy season's persistent cloud cover and moisture, which limits daytime heating and keeps highs clustered around 23–25°C according to recent Japan Meteorological Agency guidance and ensemble models. A stable high-pressure system over central Europe has suppressed cloud cover and precipitation in recent days, allowing daytime heating to exceed June climatological averages of 21–24°C. eventMetadata.context_description: A strengthening high-pressure system and associated heat dome are driving the current market positioning, with official Met Office guidance and ensemble model runs from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts pointing to peak temperatures of 34–38 °C across London on 23 June. Warm advection from the near-continent, combined with clear skies and light winds, favors rapid daytime warming, while recent amber heat-health alerts reflect growing model consensus that 35 °C will be exceeded. Forecaster updates in the past 48 hours have nudged higher-end scenarios toward 37–38 °C in parts of the southeast, yet spread among runs leaves room for a lower outcome if cloud or a slightly stronger sea breeze develops. Final guidance from the Met Office and updated high-resolution runs on 22 June will be the key catalysts likely to shift trader probabilities before resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent Met Office forecasts highlight a strengthening high-pressure system and warm southerly flow driving an early-summer heatwave across southern England, with central London temperatures potentially peaking near 35–37 °C on June 24. Ensemble model runs show modest spread around these values due to uncertain cloud cover and boundary-layer mixing, while climatological baselines place average June maxima near 20–22 °C. The amber extreme-heat warning through mid-week and the proximity to the UK June record of 35.6 °C anchor trader focus on the 36–38 °C band, though any overnight cooling or Atlantic front arrival could cap readings at 35 °C or below. Updated guidance expected within 24 hours will further refine land-breeze and urban-heat-island effects.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the Tokyo Haneda Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/jp/tokyo/RJTT.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader sentiment for Tokyo's June 23 maximum temperature centers on the tsuyu rainy season's persistent cloud cover and moisture, which limits daytime heating and keeps highs clustered around 23–25°C according to recent Japan Meteorological Agency guidance and ensemble models. Mostly cloudy skies forecast for the day reduce incoming solar radiation compared to clearer early-June conditions, while moderate humidity and light variable winds further moderate peaks near the urban heat island. Historical June averages hover near 26°C, but current atmospheric patterns favor slightly cooler outcomes, with 24°C holding the plurality of market-implied probability. Updated model runs and official briefings over the next 48 hours will refine the exact threshold before resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecast model runs from the Japan Meteorological Agency show modest disagreement on Tokyo's June 24 daytime high, producing the tight clustering of market-implied probabilities around 24–26 °C. Southerly flow ahead of an approaching trough, combined with variable cloud cover and urban heat-island effects, creates the primary uncertainty range. Ensemble guidance places the most likely maximum near the seasonal normal of roughly 25 °C, yet small shifts in timing of any coastal sea breeze or afternoon convection could easily move the observed high one degree either way. Traders are therefore weighting the latest deterministic and ensemble outputs that will be refreshed within the next 24–48 hours.

eventMetadata.context_description: Current numerical weather prediction models, including recent ECMWF and GFS runs, indicate a warm air mass advecting over southern Germany, supporting a June 23 maximum near 32 °C in Munich with light rain or showers possible midday. This consensus underpins the market’s tight clustering around 31–33 °C, where small differences in cloud timing, boundary-layer mixing, and precipitation onset create the observed spread between leading outcomes. Ensemble variability remains modest two days out, yet traders weigh the risk that earlier or heavier showers could cap the peak at 31 °C versus clearer intervals allowing 33 °C, with official DWD observations on the day serving as the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the Munich Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/de/munich/EDDM.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Forecast models from agencies like the DWD and ECMWF currently indicate a peak temperature near 32°C in Munich on June 24 under sunny skies and light northeasterly winds, supporting the market's highest implied probability on that outcome. A stable high-pressure system over central Europe has suppressed cloud cover and precipitation in recent days, allowing daytime heating to exceed June climatological averages of 21–24°C. Key variables include potential shifts in steering flow that could introduce more moisture or alter insolation, plus minor model spread on maximum values between 30–34°C. Updated short-range guidance expected in the next 24–48 hours will likely refine these projections ahead of resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: A strengthening high-pressure system and associated heat dome are driving the current market positioning, with official Met Office guidance and ensemble model runs from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts pointing to peak temperatures of 34–38 °C across London on 23 June. Warm advection from the near-continent, combined with clear skies and light winds, favors rapid daytime warming, while recent amber heat-health alerts reflect growing model consensus that 35 °C will be exceeded. Forecaster updates in the past 48 hours have nudged higher-end scenarios toward 37–38 °C in parts of the southeast, yet spread among runs leaves room for a lower outcome if cloud or a slightly stronger sea breeze develops. Final guidance from the Met Office and updated high-resolution runs on 22 June will be the key catalysts likely to shift trader probabilities before resolution.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the Tokyo Haneda Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/jp/tokyo/RJTT.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader sentiment for Tokyo's June 23 maximum temperature centers on the tsuyu rainy season's persistent cloud cover and moisture, which limits daytime heating and keeps highs clustered around 23–25°C according to recent Japan Meteorological Agency guidance and ensemble models. Mostly cloudy skies forecast for the day reduce incoming solar radiation compared to clearer early-June conditions, while moderate humidity and light variable winds further moderate peaks near the urban heat island. Historical June averages hover near 26°C, but current atmospheric patterns favor slightly cooler outcomes, with 24°C holding the plurality of market-implied probability. Updated model runs and official briefings over the next 48 hours will refine the exact threshold before resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecast model runs from the Japan Meteorological Agency show modest disagreement on Tokyo's June 24 daytime high, producing the tight clustering of market-implied probabilities around 24–26 °C. Southerly flow ahead of an approaching trough, combined with variable cloud cover and urban heat-island effects, creates the primary uncertainty range. Ensemble guidance places the most likely maximum near the seasonal normal of roughly 25 °C, yet small shifts in timing of any coastal sea breeze or afternoon convection could easily move the observed high one degree either way. Traders are therefore weighting the latest deterministic and ensemble outputs that will be refreshed within the next 24–48 hours.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent Met Office forecasts highlight a strengthening high-pressure system and warm southerly flow driving an early-summer heatwave across southern England, with central London temperatures potentially peaking near 35–37 °C on June 24. Ensemble model runs show modest spread around these values due to uncertain cloud cover and boundary-layer mixing, while climatological baselines place average June maxima near 20–22 °C. The amber extreme-heat warning through mid-week and the proximity to the UK June record of 35.6 °C anchor trader focus on the 36–38 °C band, though any overnight cooling or Atlantic front arrival could cap readings at 35 °C or below. Updated guidance expected within 24 hours will further refine land-breeze and urban-heat-island effects.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecast model runs from the Japan Meteorological Agency show modest disagreement on Tokyo's June 24 daytime high, producing the tight clustering of market-implied probabilities around 24–26 °C. Southerly flow ahead of an approaching trough, combined with variable cloud cover and urban heat-island effects, creates the primary uncertainty range. Ensemble guidance places the most likely maximum near the seasonal normal of roughly 25 °C, yet small shifts in timing of any coastal sea breeze or afternoon convection could easily move the observed high one degree either way. Traders are therefore weighting the latest deterministic and ensemble outputs that will be refreshed within the next 24–48 hours.

eventMetadata.context_description: Current numerical weather prediction models, including recent ECMWF and GFS runs, indicate a warm air mass advecting over southern Germany, supporting a June 23 maximum near 32 °C in Munich with light rain or showers possible midday. This consensus underpins the market’s tight clustering around 31–33 °C, where small differences in cloud timing, boundary-layer mixing, and precipitation onset create the observed spread between leading outcomes. Ensemble variability remains modest two days out, yet traders weigh the risk that earlier or heavier showers could cap the peak at 31 °C versus clearer intervals allowing 33 °C, with official DWD observations on the day serving as the resolution source.

eventMetadata.context_description: Forecast models from NOAA and other agencies indicate a high-pressure ridge over the Northeast with light northerly flow and variable cloud cover moderating peak heating in New York City on June 23, keeping the daily maximum in the mid-to-upper 70s°F rather than the seasonal average near 80–83°F. Ensemble spreads arise mainly from differences in predicted boundary-layer mixing, dew points, and the timing of any weak frontal passage, producing the tight clustering of market-implied odds around 76–81°F. Recent short-range guidance updates have shown minimal day-to-day shifts, reflecting stable large-scale steering patterns and limited convective activity that could otherwise boost or suppress temperatures by a few degrees. Traders are weighting these consensus outputs heavily, as resolution will hinge on official Central Park observations.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent National Weather Service and model guidance for New York City point to a highest temperature on June 24 near the 82–85 °F range, reflecting a mild airmass with limited warm advection and possible moderating sea-breeze influence along the coast. Ensemble spreads and differences between deterministic runs create the tight clustering of market-implied probabilities around those bins, as small shifts in timing of any cloud cover or boundary-layer mixing can alter the daily maximum by a degree or two. This pattern aligns with typical late-June climatology for Central Park, where highs average near 82 °F, while the absence of strong high-pressure ridging or upstream heat sources keeps the upper tail of the distribution low. Forecasters will issue final updates through the overnight and morning hours before resolution.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the Munich Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/de/munich/EDDM.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Forecast models from agencies like the DWD and ECMWF currently indicate a peak temperature near 32°C in Munich on June 24 under sunny skies and light northeasterly winds, supporting the market's highest implied probability on that outcome. A stable high-pressure system over central Europe has suppressed cloud cover and precipitation in recent days, allowing daytime heating to exceed June climatological averages of 21–24°C. Key variables include potential shifts in steering flow that could introduce more moisture or alter insolation, plus minor model spread on maximum values between 30–34°C. Updated short-range guidance expected in the next 24–48 hours will likely refine these projections ahead of resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: A strengthening high-pressure system and associated heat dome are driving the current market positioning, with official Met Office guidance and ensemble model runs from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts pointing to peak temperatures of 34–38 °C across London on 23 June. Warm advection from the near-continent, combined with clear skies and light winds, favors rapid daytime warming, while recent amber heat-health alerts reflect growing model consensus that 35 °C will be exceeded. Forecaster updates in the past 48 hours have nudged higher-end scenarios toward 37–38 °C in parts of the southeast, yet spread among runs leaves room for a lower outcome if cloud or a slightly stronger sea breeze develops. Final guidance from the Met Office and updated high-resolution runs on 22 June will be the key catalysts likely to shift trader probabilities before resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent Met Office forecasts highlight a strengthening high-pressure system and warm southerly flow driving an early-summer heatwave across southern England, with central London temperatures potentially peaking near 35–37 °C on June 24. Ensemble model runs show modest spread around these values due to uncertain cloud cover and boundary-layer mixing, while climatological baselines place average June maxima near 20–22 °C. The amber extreme-heat warning through mid-week and the proximity to the UK June record of 35.6 °C anchor trader focus on the 36–38 °C band, though any overnight cooling or Atlantic front arrival could cap readings at 35 °C or below. Updated guidance expected within 24 hours will further refine land-breeze and urban-heat-island effects.

eventMetadata.context_description: Forecast models from NOAA and other agencies indicate a high-pressure ridge over the Northeast with light northerly flow and variable cloud cover moderating peak heating in New York City on June 23, keeping the daily maximum in the mid-to-upper 70s°F rather than the seasonal average near 80–83°F. Ensemble spreads arise mainly from differences in predicted boundary-layer mixing, dew points, and the timing of any weak frontal passage, producing the tight clustering of market-implied odds around 76–81°F. Recent short-range guidance updates have shown minimal day-to-day shifts, reflecting stable large-scale steering patterns and limited convective activity that could otherwise boost or suppress temperatures by a few degrees. Traders are weighting these consensus outputs heavily, as resolution will hinge on official Central Park observations.

eventMetadata.context_description: Current numerical weather prediction models, including recent ECMWF and GFS runs, indicate a warm air mass advecting over southern Germany, supporting a June 23 maximum near 32 °C in Munich with light rain or showers possible midday. This consensus underpins the market’s tight clustering around 31–33 °C, where small differences in cloud timing, boundary-layer mixing, and precipitation onset create the observed spread between leading outcomes. Ensemble variability remains modest two days out, yet traders weigh the risk that earlier or heavier showers could cap the peak at 31 °C versus clearer intervals allowing 33 °C, with official DWD observations on the day serving as the resolution source.

eventMetadata.context_description: Forecast models from agencies like the DWD and ECMWF currently indicate a peak temperature near 32°C in Munich on June 24 under sunny skies and light northeasterly winds, supporting the market's highest implied probability on that outcome. A stable high-pressure system over central Europe has suppressed cloud cover and precipitation in recent days, allowing daytime heating to exceed June climatological averages of 21–24°C. Key variables include potential shifts in steering flow that could introduce more moisture or alter insolation, plus minor model spread on maximum values between 30–34°C. eventMetadata.context_description: Current forecasts from sources including the BBC and numerical models indicate sunny conditions under high pressure for Munich on June 25, supporting peak temperatures near 33°C, with some guidance reaching 34°C. This drives the near-tie between those two outcomes at 34.5% implied probability each. Differentiation hinges on factors such as precise timing of maximum insolation, boundary-layer mixing, light easterly winds limiting or enhancing local heating, and minor model spread in 2-meter temperatures. Recent runs show limited day-to-day shifts, though afternoon cloud development or slight advection changes could tip the daily high by 1°C. Updated short-range guidance expected within 24 hours will refine resolution criteria around official station readings.

eventMetadata.context_description: A strengthening high-pressure system and associated heat dome are driving the current market positioning, with official Met Office guidance and ensemble model runs from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts pointing to peak temperatures of 34–38 °C across London on 23 June. Warm advection from the near-continent, combined with clear skies and light winds, favors rapid daytime warming, while recent amber heat-health alerts reflect growing model consensus that 35 °C will be exceeded. Forecaster updates in the past 48 hours have nudged higher-end scenarios toward 37–38 °C in parts of the southeast, yet spread among runs leaves room for a lower outcome if cloud or a slightly stronger sea breeze develops. Final guidance from the Met Office and updated high-resolution runs on 22 June will be the key catalysts likely to shift trader probabilities before resolution.

This market will resolve to the temperature range that contains the highest temperature recorded at the Tokyo Haneda Airport Station in degrees Celsius on 24 Jun '26.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the Tokyo Haneda Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/jp/tokyo/RJTT.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecast model runs from the Japan Meteorological Agency show modest disagreement on Tokyo's June 24 daytime high, producing the tight clustering of market-implied probabilities around 24–26 °C. Southerly flow ahead of an approaching trough, combined with variable cloud cover and urban heat-island effects, creates the primary uncertainty range. Ensemble guidance places the most likely maximum near the seasonal normal of roughly 25 °C, yet small shifts in timing of any coastal sea breeze or afternoon convection could easily move the observed high one degree either way. Traders are therefore weighting the latest deterministic and ensemble outputs that will be refreshed within the next 24–48 hours.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent Japan Meteorological Agency forecasts highlight persistent cloud cover and a 90% chance of rain for Tokyo on June 25, which should reduce solar heating and cap the daily maximum near 25–27 °C under the influence of the early-summer tsuyu pattern. These conditions create genuine uncertainty around peak temperature, as thicker cloud decks or heavier showers could suppress readings by 2–4 °C while clearer breaks might allow brief warming. Market-implied odds reflect this narrow range, with the tight clustering between 22 °C and 24 °C underscoring traders’ assessment of moisture-driven variability and the modest historical precedent for mid-20s highs during comparable late-June setups. Updated model runs expected within 24 hours could shift probabilities if guidance diverges on precipitation timing.
